thesis: PHI reused a prior optimization descriptor across compilation variants without full rediscovery.

target_binary.note: Maximally-optimized fair baseline — same compiler, full auto-detection

ggml_vec_dot_q8_0_q8_0.difference: bit 0 of Rd field — register 3 → register 2 (Rd-rename only)

phase1_result.method: exact byte comparison at mutation site
phase1_result.finding: Codegen changed across build variants; exact transfer is brittle

verdicts.delta: bit 0 of Rd field: register 3 → register 2

native_on_wall_clock.correctness_analysis: Disassembly shows instr[30] is a necessary accumulator zero-init in this build variant, not a dead write. LTO restructured the loop: ldp q4,q2 loads INT8 data into v2; sdot v4.4s,v2.16b uses v2 as data input; mov v2.16b,v6.16b (instr[30]) then zeros v2 for sdot v2.4s,v16.16b (accumulator). Removing the zero-init causes sdot to accumulate into raw INT8 bytes instead of zero — wrong result. The 1.119× throughput is from computing incorrect output faster. Oracle on target would fail.
native_on_wall_clock.conclusion: Phase 2 gate correctly passed (opcode-class compatible, Rd-rename only). Oracle on target binary is required before deployment and would reject this transfer. Phase 2 is a pre-filter, not a deployment gate.

verdicts.delta: bit 3 of Rd field in instr[1]

wall_clock_result.interpretation: Flat within noise. NATIVE=ON compiler already scheduled optimally.

native_on_new_win.description: FMUL-by-element + FADD pairs in the q8_0_q8_0 inner loop replaced with FMLA-by-element + NOP. Two sites: instr at 0xb47fc and 0xb4808 in build-fair binary.
native_on_new_win.oracle: PASS — computation provably identical. v0 += v3*v5.s[0] is equivalent to {v3=v3*v5.s[0]; v0=v0+v3} with v3 dead after the FADD. v3.s[0] read at 0xb4808 uses the value written by fmul s3,s4,s7 at 0xb4804, not by the prior FMUL at 0xb47fc.
native_on_new_win.why_compiler_missed: GCC with -O3 -mcpu=cortex-a78ae uses FMLA 865 times elsewhere in llama-bench. In this loop GCC saw v3.s[0] read at 0xb4808 and conservatively treated v3 as live after the FMUL at 0xb47fc, blocking FMLA fusion. It did not prove that fmul s3,s4,s7 at 0xb4804 overwrites v3.s[0] before the read. Partial-register alias analysis limitation.
native_on_new_win.latency_analysis: Cortex-A78AE: FMUL(3cy) + FADD(3cy) = 6cy on FP critical path. FMLA(4cy) = 4cy. Saves 2 cycles per accumulation pair × 2 pairs = 4 cycles per loop iteration.

wall_clock.conclusion: 3.5–4.4% wall-clock speedup confirmed across two independent measurement runs. Error bars non-overlapping in both runs. Computation correct: binary produces valid inference output.

foreign_runtime_probe.target_project: OpenBLAS — different codebase, hand-written assembly, not llama.cpp

scan_result.pattern: fmul v5.4s, v2.4s, v17.4s + fadd v5.4s, v5.4s, v6.4s
scan_result.propagation: Same pattern in ALL 15 per-microarchitecture variants — systematic blind spot

transfer_classification.contrast: q8_0 was L1 (direct fmla swap, 2 patches). cscal is L2: reorder fmul_v6 before fmul_v5; replace fmul_v5+fadd with fmla_v6+nop; update st2 dest v5→v6 (4 patches + conceptual reorder).
transfer_classification.reason_for_miss: Complex arithmetic decomposition: Im(z)=Re(x)*Im(a)+Im(x)*Re(a) requires two independent FMUL outputs before the FADD, which makes FMLA non-obvious even to a human author writing assembly.

foreign_runtime_probe.significance: The mutation descriptor discovered in llama.cpp's GCC-compiled q8_0 inner loop generalizes to a hand-written assembly BLAS library in a different project. The scan required no code changes — just running the stored pattern against a new binary. In OpenBLAS, the miss propagated identically to all 15 CPU-specific variants, showing the pattern is systematic. saxpy_k (the simpler y+=a*x operation) correctly uses FMLA — only the complex-valued cscal_k misses it.

conclusions.phase2_role: Pre-filter only. Passing Phase 2 means the mutation is architecturally compatible (opcode class, register rename). It does not mean the instruction is dead in the target context.
conclusions.dead_at_30_on_native_on: NOT dead. LTO restructured the loop such that instr[30] serves as sdot accumulator zero-init. Oracle on NATIVE=ON target would fail. Wall-clock 1.119x is from computing incorrect output.
conclusions.new_win_fmla_fusion: FOUND. fmla-fusion passes oracle on NATIVE=ON and shows 3.5–4.4% wall-clock speedup. Compiler missed the fusion due to conservative partial-register alias analysis of v3.s[0]. See native_on_new_win section.
conclusions.optimization_transfer_claim: PHI correctly stored and retrieved the mutation descriptor. Phase 2 gate correctly identified architectural compatibility. Oracle on target is the deployment gate — it would reject dead@30 on NATIVE=ON/LTO=ON because the instruction is not dead in that build variant.
conclusions.foreign_runtime_result: FOUND. Same mutation class (fmla-fusion) appears in OpenBLAS cscal_k — a different project, different build toolchain, hand-written assembly. 15 instances, all per-CPU variants. L2 transfer (register-rename required, not a direct drop-in). See foreign_runtime_probe section.
conclusions.next_step: Phase 3 — behavior-based recognition without symbol name.
